full_response:
We work in the rest frame of the Earth (and of the planet, which we assume is at rest relative to Earth).

1. Proper time measured on the spaceship  
The ship’s clock reads a total of  
 τtotal = 5 yr  
of which one year is spent sitting on the planet (no relative motion, so no dilation).  
 τstay = 1 yr  
Therefore the proper time accumulated while the ship is in flight is  

 τflight = τtotal – τstay = 5 yr – 1 yr = 4 yr  
 τflight = τout + τin .                (1)

2. Time dilation on each leg  
Set c = 1 so that speeds are dimensionless and distances come out in light-years (ly).

Outbound speed: vout = 0.9  
 γout = 1/√(1 – vout²) = 1/√(1 – 0.9²) = 2.2942  

Inbound speed: vin = 0.99  
 γin = 1/√(1 – vin²) = 7.0888  

The ship’s proper times and the coordinate times measured on Earth are related by  

 tout = γout τout          (2a)  
 tin  = γin  τin           (2b)

3. Distance to the planet  
In the Earth frame the distance D (in light-years) satisfies  

 D = vout tout = vin tin.             (3)

Using (2) in (3):

 D = vout γout τout = vin γin τin.       (4)

Let  

 A ≡ vout γout = 0.9 (2.2942) = 2.0647  
 B ≡ vin  γin = 0.99 (7.0888) = 7.0170

Then  

 τout = D/A , τin = D/B.            (5)

Insert (5) into the proper–time sum (1):

 D/A + D/B = 4 yr  
 D (1/A + 1/B) = 4 yr  
 D = 4 yr / (1/A + 1/B).            (6)

Compute  

 1/A = 0.4842  1/B = 0.1425  
 1/A + 1/B = 0.6267

Therefore  

 D = 4 / 0.6267 ≈ 6.39 ly.          (7)

4. Convert to light-seconds  
1 light-year = 365.25 d × 24 h × 3600 s ≈ 3.15576 × 10⁷ s.

 D = 6.39 ly × 3.15576 × 10⁷ s/ly  
  ≈ 2.02 × 10⁸ light-seconds.            

Final result
\boxed{D \;\approx\; 2.0 \times 10^{8}\;\text{light-seconds}\; \left(\;6.4\;\text{light-years}\;\right)}

Confidence: 83%
